speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. And it's tape number 12. And we are speaking about the conditions in Mauthausen and how difficult it was and that you got weaker, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't in such a good condition anymore. Now, what-- how-- how did it go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were losing strength and, in the periods in between, I would like to tell you a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: Examples.

speaker: interviewee
text: Examples which I remember-- how the conditions were between us children, maybe from this group, we were about eight or 10, trying to stick together. Once, one of us took something or stole from a woman, from this group of people who arrived from Hungary. And in our eyes, that wasn't correct. And our punishment was that we didn't spoke with him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't speak with him for one or two days. We put him in [NON-ENGLISH], in a ban. And this was so unbearable for him, that he went to a kapo and sold himself to a homosexual couple. Everyone knew about it, that he, for this service, gives them a loaf of bread. He brought us the loaf of bread as if a kind of reconciliation with us, asking for forgiveness. I don't remember if we took it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: The question is, did you accept him back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We accept him back, but if we also took the bread or let him eat  it alone, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But his thing was that he took something from a woman, and it was against your law.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right-- against our unwritten law. There were other things in Mauthausen. The conditions were very, very, bad, as I told you. And the soup was distributed for the children in garbage container. And these garbage containers were loaded on, again, a kind of roll wagon, on wagons. And of course, of the terrain, some of the drops of soup were spilled out. And I remember a elderly-- in our eyes, elderly, at that time it's man of 30 or 40-- went with his kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pot.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pot he had, and tried-- from underneath the wagon, these dirty drops of soup, which runs through the thing-- to collect it, a few drops. SS man saw it. I saw the whole thing. He took him to a open ditch where many-- where they were throwing people who died and, also, people who were executed on the spot. He told him, stay before the ditch, and shot him in my presence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do? He took some drops of soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some drops of soup, which were running through this dirty carriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the punishment. Later on, we came, as I told you, to the [INAUDIBLE] lager. And afterwards, there were, again, a kind of selection for further march, another death transport. And we were marched out of Mauthausen. And that was late April, in  the late of the April. And after many days of marching, which, again, were one of the worst experiences I had in my career in [INAUDIBLE]. We marched through-- thought villages and beautiful Austrian--

speaker: interviewer
text: Area.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not allowed to take--

speaker: interviewer
text: Landscape.

speaker: interviewee
text: --water from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Villages.

speaker: interviewee
text: --villages and so on. I remember one night, we marched, and I saw in a little villa, in a little house, through the window, people sitting on a table, the shadows of a couple sitting on the table. And that, after Auschwitz and all, was unbelievable. It was like a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Normal life.

speaker: interviewee
text: That there is still-- yeah-- that there are still people sitting on a table, on chairs. I mean, I wasn't sitting on a table and a chair for, at that time, I think, two years or so. I can't forget it. And the SS men who accompanied us, they changed. I mean, now, they didn't have any more young SS men. And they had these elderly people, around 50, 60, which they mobilized for this kind of job. They were not anymore so cruel as the SS. But they were still very tough. And we came to a-- into a forest, to Gunskirchen. That was the last station. And here, people died like flies from typhoid, from diarrhea, just dying, dying, dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your condition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. I-- I knew that-- there was rumors the war is over, and soon there will be a kind of liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- no, we were too weak to enjoy it or so. Weeks before, I remember, in Mauthausen, when we had a little rest, and we children could sit somehow-- so we daydreamed. What was our daydream? What shall we do after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: So first of all, many of them, maybe all of us-- that we take revenge. Revenge. There were two things we children wanted to do. First of-- first, uberleben, to over-- To live.

speaker: interviewee
text: To live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: To survive the Germans. That as if, would be the greatest revenge.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the biggest-- this is the-- the biggest revenge. It's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second revenge-- how to take revenge on what they have done to us. So in our children fantasies, we said we will build a huge wall all around Germany and let them starve. That was one thing. Then, the other dreams were how wonderful it will be. There will be no gas. We will be free. We couldn't imagine. We were always in hoards, and even in the toilets, always we had other hundreds of people. Toilets-- you know how it was in Auschwitz and in Mauthausen-- just impossible conditions. But we thought we will be alone. It was our greatest dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: To sit alone in a toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or to have a potato. Of course, I didn't dreamt I want a boiled potato, just a raw potato for ourselves. Or to have one egg for ourself. That was our greatest dream--

speaker: interviewer
text: Daydream.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be alone, to be left alone--

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone in a toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: --without the guard, and to have this one potato and enough food. Well, in-- now I go back to Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. This was in Mauthausen, when you were still stronger, strong enough to speak--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could-- And we could speak about-- in Mauthausen,

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] --we were very, very, very weak. We still killed, daily, about 200 lices.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lice. And we used, of course, the language of the German. So we said, after counting to 100, I have done enough for our nation. I've done enough for-- or Hitler or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: For Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- I've done my duty enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were full of lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And they all, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some hair, which were growing after Auschwitz because, you must understand, we couldn't change in the last two, three months our clothes or have water to wash. We were sleeping in the old rags, sometimes on the street. But strangely enough, we never caught a cold. My first cold-- I caught a cold after the war when I just dressed as a normal human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you got typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: After two days of the liberation, I got typhoid. But this kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: --slang we used-- I mean, now, about when we killed all the louse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- the lice. We were completely full of lice. And we couldn't even sit in these overpopulated barracks where they put hundreds of people. We couldn't sleep. We couldn't sit. I remember my friend and I, we somehow climbed on this wood construction and bound ourself-- we shouldn't fell down-- because it was very dangerous. But at least we were alone on one little piece of wood--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because between the other people, it was impossible. You were squeezed or-- you couldn't do anything. And if you went outside, just in the  mud, the possibility was you were-- you died in this mud. And around, people were just dying, just dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got no food whatsoever?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- and there was no food anymore. All the people who were a bit stronger, they grabbed it. Sometimes you got few crumbs, but it was hopeless. Then, I think it was Friday night on the 4th or 5th of May, we heard the SS left the camp. And we were looking around. And everyone was running to the place where they distributed the food. And everyone tried to grab something. I remember I grabbed some cube of marge, and I put it in my pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you grab?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margarine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Margarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Margarine and maybe a few pieces of sugar, and-- and a piece of bread, also. But they came-- stronger, Russian or other prisoners-- and I tried to keep my pockets that they shouldn't take it away. But again, I was saved in two ways. If I would eat the marge, I would die because we couldn't eat any fat things.

speaker: interviewer
text: The margarine saved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If-- they saved me that they stole it from me. And how they stole it-- we were very clever, and didn't let him. But he took a kind of razor and cut the pocket and took it out. And then, we knew the war is over. There was a tremendous [INAUDIBLE] and screaming. But it was night. And we decided, next morning, a friend of mine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. We heard them. And the next morning, we decided we will go. Now, we had two options. Most of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one who you-- who was with you on the bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the friends went back from the little towns they came from-- Lambach, Wels, and so on. That was on the way from where we came. But we decided we will go a different direction. And again, in a way, this saved us. Because we said, we will go to Switzerland-- we hadn't idea it's hundreds of kilometers away-- and in Switzerland is a international Red Cross, there we will have some connection with Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were a Musulmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's true. I will explain you soon what happened. And there, we will get some food parcels. And we decided to go in a different way. Now, two of us went out of the camp. We had these few pieces of bread and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And now, the first group we met were some Russian ex-- strong VIP soldiers. He said, davai! And they stole us the bread from us. We had, still, the mentality of [INAUDIBLE] people. What we could put in our mouth-- I think it was a piece of sugar-- we swallowed. But the bread, we couldn't. We were very sorry. But later on, we knew that, like in many other camps, the bread was poisoned. So again, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who poisoned the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans have done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people who ate the bread died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Even if we would eat the marge, we would die because we weren't used--

speaker: interviewer
text: The margarine was also poisoned?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the margarine wasn't, but the margarine was fat. That was the ration for 20--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very bad for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your condition-- in your condition, you couldn't eat margarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, what happened, here comes the first--

speaker: interviewer
text: But sugar, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sugar, one little piece of sugar, I could swallow. Now comes the first group of Americans, the first tanks, or whatever it was, and lorries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are two little boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two little boys. We didn't know that the American--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you weigh?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we knew later, in the hospital, we were just 33, 34 kilos. And we found a Jewish soldier. And he was forbidden to take this infected ex-prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now there was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: But this one was a Jewish soldier, American, Stanley Lipa, [? Shlomo ?] Lipa. And we spoke few words of English. We said, bread, bread, bread. He said, no, I'm sorry. We don't have bread. We are the very first ones. A kitchen will come later. But we have some cakes. And he gave us a little tin of cheese. And we thought, oh, what nonsense. It's a tiny, little tin of cheese. And we are so starved. And-- but the truth of the matter is, we couldn't even swallow one bite of this little cheese because it was not for our stomach. And this soldier saved us again because he took us to nearby village. And there was a German family. He threw them out. And he put us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Lambach. It was near Gunskirchen. And he took us-- the very first time, I could sleep. I remember it was in a children's bed. It was so small and thin. He put us in. But a few hours afterwards, we got a very high fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was with you? He stayed with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he somehow communicated with us. And he took us--

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was against the law of the American--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was against the law, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --army. And what was the reason for this law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were infected. We had typhoid. We didn't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were afraid that the soldiers would get it from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he took us to the hospital, to nearby [INAUDIBLE], a very big city, in the town hospital, city hospital. And there, he told to the German doctor, if these two boys don't live, I shoot you. I remember, we [INAUDIBLE]. And he said-- anyhow, we got--

speaker: interviewer
text: He put you both--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a normal hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a normal hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the luck was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, I want to ask you, did your friend also get typhoid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: It happened simultaneously?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them got typhoid. But our luck was that we could survive the typhoid in a normal condition. We were only two--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes and you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --ex-prisoners in a hospital. The others were hundreds, or maybe thousands in a over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Crowded area.

speaker: interviewee
text: --crowded area without any possibility to give to every one of us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Help.

speaker: interviewee
text: Help, or the adequate help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, many of them died after the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of course. Many, many of them died. It was enough that they got a-- a kind of conserve of food. And they swallowed it, and they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't. I-- We were lucky because of the soldier. And we got-- But in that hospital, did you get a good--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got a normal, good treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were. We had, I remember, 10 or 12 days, a very high fever-- 40, 41. But we survived it. And they were Catholic nurses who nursed us back to life. And I remember very well, the very first day I could go out of bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were German? There were-- they were Austrian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I guess, Austrian nurses. And I looked in a mirror after many years, and it was very unpleasant. I said, it's impossible, such ugly and terrible face. I was shocked to see my face. But slowly, we recovered. And we had enough strength to continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you keep in contact with this American soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is a story for itself. He helped us, first of all, to be able to continue our way back to Prague. He wrote some letters of recommendation and so on, which helped us. Why? Because the normal procedure was, at the time of the war, from the American zone, to go to the Russian zone. And from the Russian zone-- I think that time Vienna was under the Russian occupation-- we could take our way to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? First of all, they took all us in a camp after the war. Yeah. There is also one beautiful story from Gunskirchen. Near Gunskirchen was a train of the German soldiers who just left the train and escaped. But we children-- the American soldiers-- I remember the very first negro I saw. I mean, today you say the black-- In the American army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Army. Let us go in the train. And we children knew it's dangerous to eat something. So first of all, we were looking for medication.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is good that you knew that. Who told you that you are not allowed to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew it-- we knew it. The danger was in the camp to get dysentery. That means a death. So we were looking for the train with medication. There are many, many wagons. And I remember even the name of the medication, which was, at that time, against dysentery was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ate a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] before we ate something else. We found a lot of food there. Was it after--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was, more or less, in this-- at this time. I can't tell you exactly. But that was the first time after--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came, as I told you, to a-- again, to a camp, from where most of them went to the Russian side, then they went till Slovakia and slowly reached, after a couple of weeks, Prague. There was no other way. There was a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We two children wrote a list-- pro and contra-- what will happen if we run away from the camp-- it was now after the war-- and go straight on our own, and what will happen if we don't. So we know if don't, it will be-- we have to go again, all this with a group and with the Russian soldiers. But if we do it by our own, we have a chance, maybe a little better. And that what we choose. We somehow escaped, went by our own to Vienna. And then, there, we went to the Czech consul. Now, in Vienna, the first repatriation cars left from Prague, let's say, once or twice in a-- in a week. And all these repatriation people who went back and forwards, were actually black market people. We were the very first real repatriation--

speaker: interviewer
text: Refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Refugees and children. So these few days, we were staying in a Czech family, [INAUDIBLE], I remember was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the Czech consul, most probably they gave us this address.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. There was already a Czech consulate--

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been because they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --working in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we came to Prague in the early morning, my friend and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, on these lorries. They were not trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: On these lorries, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know what to do. But my friend was from Prague, so we went to the Jewish community in the Meisel, street number 18. There was nothing prepared for the children. These people came, were liberated recently from Theresienstadt. And they just rebuilt or reorganized the life of the Jewish community, whatever was left. There were no children from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were refugees coming in already to Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were the first refugees coming from Theresienstadt or from other camps. And here, we have been the first children.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nothing for the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one to take care for children.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We asked, what shall we do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told us, we have nothing ready. We just arrived ourselves. But there is one man, a wonderful Czech, who thought about it, that you children come back. And he has a kind of home-- recreation home for children. And you will go there. And he will take care of you. And so it was. And this man was one of the most extraordinary men I-- well, in modern term. You would call him angel, who helped us enormously.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Premysl Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Premysl Peter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Premysl Peter in Czech. And he was a social worker who was very well known in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish. He belonged to the Czech Brothers, this is special, elite Protestant group, the best of the Czech tradition, which goes back to the Czech Brothers many hundreds years ago. And he thought about it, that children may come back from the camps. He thought that there are many children in Theresienstadt because he knew these Jewish children who went to Theresienstadt. One of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever visit Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, during?

speaker: interviewee
text: During nobody could visit. It was a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody could visit. But during the war, in '40, '41, he, with the help of his friends, organized food, mainly milk, to-- into Jewish families where there were newborn babies because the Germans didn't allow to give--

speaker: interviewer
text: Milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: --milk to Jewish newborn babies. And he was denounced. And he got called to the chief of Gestapo in Prague. But when the chief of Gestapo asked him, is it true, Mr. Peter, that you are distributing milk? Don't you know that it's forbidden? So instead, like all of them, he didn't say, well, I didn't, or so. He said, of course. It's my human duty. And that made such an impression on the chief of the Gestapo that he let him free-- not only in this case, but all during the war, he was somehow free from the persecution of the Gestapo. This man took over former German castles in the neighborhood of Prague-- the castle of [NON-ENGLISH] and so on, which belonged to a German aristocracy. The castle we have been as children, belonged to the former minister of finance and he, with the help of his friends, changed it into recreation homes. Therefore, again, we were tremendously lucky to come in a peaceful surrounding with lovely people who really cared for us. The other children came to DP camps, sometimes for--

speaker: interviewer
text: DP [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camps years. Or they had at once to struggle and care for themselves and get the ration cards and food, et cetera, et cetera. That was the world after liberation in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we arrived there in the middle of-- or the end of June-- the middle-- I think the end of June. And slowly, we-- through the better food and enormously human and lovely care--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a staff, Peter?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had, of course, a staff of people who helped him. He was a kind of represent of the Ministry of Social Welfare, and therefore, he had a free hand to go, after the war, to all the camps and take the most needed children-- not only Jewish but Czech and German children who were, at that time, also in camps-- and bring them over. And in this way, he helped us. I've been in contact with him all these years, till he died, in-- I think in '74 or so. And we children managed, also, that he got a medal of the Jewish State and the tree--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Yad Vashem for the Righteous One of the non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So he's a righteous--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really, we should stop now, and we shall soon go on.
